items.title: Basting stitch
items.description:
A basting stitch is a temporary stitch to hold layers of fabric together and it will later be removed.
Using a thread color that contrasts with your fabric will make the basting thread more visible for easy removal.

items.title: Running stitch
items.description:
The straight or running stitch is the basic stitch in hand-sewing and embroidery, on which all other forms of sewing are based. The stitch is worked by passing the needle in and out of the fabric at a regular distance.
Sew a line of running stitches by inserting the needle and thread through both layers of fabric a short distance from your starting point. Bring the needle and thread up to the top of the layers the same distance as the first stitch and keep repeating until you reach the end.

items.title: Back stitch
items.description:
A backstitch is a basic hand stitch that creates a line of stitches without a break between them, so it looks like a continuous straight line of thread, it is also one of the most durable stitches that doesn’t come undone easily.
The stitch is made by bringing thread behind the previous stitch and then coming out of the fabric in front of the previous stitch.

items.title: Chain stitch
items.description:
Chain stitch is a sewing and embroidery technique in which a series of looped stitches form a chain-like pattern.
Because of its continuous looped nature, the stitch easily comes apart so it is more suitable for decorative purposes than functional purposes like holding two or more fabric layers together.
Bring knotted needle to front of fabric. Pull thread through. Insert needle into fabric a tiny distance below where thread came out previously. Bring needle out from fabric to create a loop. Bring the needle to front a stitch length away from the starting point. Pull thread through to form first loop of the chain.
Continue forming loops in the same way.

items.title: Ladder stitch
items.description:
To sew a ladder stitch, start by threading a needle with matching thread and tying a small knot at the end. Begin by inserting the needle into one edge and sew a stitch then insert the needle into the other edge and sew a stitch with the same length, Continue alternating stitches between the two edges, making sure they are spaced evenly. Once you've reached the end, secure the thread with two or three knots and hide the thread tail inside the project.
The ladder stitch is also called the slip stitch or the invisible stitch or the blind stitch.

items.title: Blanket stitch
items.description:
A blanket stitch is a simple and decorative stitch often used for finishing edges of fabric or creating a neat hem.

1. Thread the needle and tie a small knot at the end.

2. Make a loop with the first stitch and pass needle under the loop over fabric edge as shown in the tutorial, I like to do this twice for a neat first stitch.

3. To sew the first stitch, insert the needle a stitch length away from the start point and slightly below the edge of the fabric, keeping the needle pointing up (towards fabric edge). Loop thread behind the needle and pull the needle through (up). This is your first blanket stitch.

items.title: Overlock stitch
items.description:
The overlock stitch is a variation of the blanket stitch. It’s both decorative and secure, making it perfect for finishing the edges of blankets, garments, and home textiles.

In this tutorial, I'll share my unique method for starting the stitch, which creates a perfect triangle as the first stitch. Let’s call it the 

To sew an overlock stitch by hand, use double thread. The stitches are formed by passing the needle between the threads and pulling the thread to tighten the loop, creating a triangle shape.

Repeat the steps until the edging is finished.

items.title: Catch stitch
items.description:
A catch stitch is a common hand sewing technique used for hemming, which creates a zigzag pattern on the underside of the fabric. It is strong and flexible, making it suitable for both non stretch and stretch fabrics, and is nearly invisible on the outside of the garment.

Before sewing the catch stitch, finish the raw fabric edges, then fold and press the hem at the seam allowance. Use a matching thread to sew the catch stitch.

A catch stitch can also be called a flat catch stitch. This technique involves taking a small amount of fabric from each side of the seam or hem, creating a zigzag pattern that lies flat against the fabric, resulting in a clean, professional finish.

items.title: Blind hem stitch
items.description:
A blind hem stitch is used to create a hem that is not visible once finished, It's quick and simple and it joins two pieces of fabric with practically invisible seams.

Use a matching thread and a single thread for lightweight fabrics and double thread for medium weight fabrics.

Fold the hem twice, insert the needle into the folded edge and hide the knot.
Take 1 or 2 thread from the wrong side and pull the needle through. Insert the needle into the folded edge and sew a small stitch.

Repeat until you reach the end of the hem. Tie the thread into the loop of the last stitch and hide the thread tail and trim the excess thread.

items.title: Buttonhole Stitch
items.description:
Draw a rectangle 2 mm × the length of the button  (this is the inner rectangle).

Next, draw a second rectangle so that each side is 2 mm away from the sides of the first rectangle (this will be the outer rectangle). 

Use a running stitch and sew along the edges of the outer rectangle to create a border for the buttonhole.

Then, fill the gap between the inner rectangle and the outer rectangle as shown in the videos.

Afterward, use a seam ripper to carefully open the buttonhole.

items.title: Start a stitch
items.description:
This is my go-to method for starting a stitch: 
Untwist the thread, tie a single knot at the end, and pass the needle through the loop formed by the double thread at the back (wrong side) before pulling it tight.

items.title: End a stitch
items.description:
Complete your final stitch and bring the needle to the WRONG SIDE of the fabric.
On the wrong side, pass the needle through the last stitch you made to create a small loop, and then pass the needle through the loop to form the first knot. Pull it tight to secure it.
Create a second knot by forming another small loop and passing the needle through it, then pulling it tight.
Once you're sure the knots are secure, trim any excess thread.

items.title: Hide the thread end #1
items.description:
How to hide the thread end after knotting and before cutting it off - Method #1
Depending on your project, you can choose this method, method #2 , or simply clip the thread after tying a knot at the end of the stitch.

items.title: Hide the thread end #2
items.description:
How to hide the thread end after knotting and before cutting it off  - Method #2

Insert the needle close to the knot, between the fabric layers away from the knot. Make sure the thread does not catch the outside of the fabric. Bring the needle up, away from the knot, and gently pull the thread to create a small gathering. Then, trim off the excess thread.

This technique effectively hides the thread ends, creating a clean, professional finish. 

Depending on your project, you can choose this method, Method #1, or simply clip the thread after tying a knot at the end of the stitch.

items.title: Open plain seam
items.description:
Sew the fabric pieces right sides together, finish the raw edges of the seam allowances separately, and press the seam open.
This technique is ideal for garments, quilting, and projects where a clean, flat seam is needed for a professional finish.

items.title: Closed plain seam
items.description:
Sew the fabric pieces right sides together, finish the raw edges of the seam allowances together, and press the seam to one side.

You can use this technique for garments like shirts, dresses, and pants to achieve a professional finish.

items.title: Edgestitch seam
items.description:
An edgestitch seam is a topstitch sewn close to the edge of the fabric, typically 1.5 to 3 mm away. It’s both decorative and functional, as it flattens seams and reinforces the edges.

To sew an edgestitch seam, use an edge stitching foot or the inside edge of the standard presser foot as a guide. With the right side of the fabric facing up, sew close to the seam, ensuring you catch the seam allowance on the wrong side.

Edgestitching is commonly used on garments to provide a clean finish. It’s also ideal for securing seam allowances in place, or for decorative accents in home décor items like pillowcases or tablecloths.

items.title: Topstitch seam
items.description:
A topstitch seam is a decorative or functional stitching line sewn on the right side of a garment, parallel to a seam.
It is often used to reinforce seams, add definition, or create a finished look.
Topstitching is usually sewn about 6mm (or 1/4 inch) away from the seam line. You can also use the outer edge of your standard presser foot as a guide.

items.title: Welt Seam
items.description:
A welt seam is a strong, flat seam often used for medium to heavy-weight fabrics. It resembles a flat-felled seam but is less bulky, making it ideal for jeans, denim, and other garments that require durability.

To sew a welt seam:
Place the fabric right sides together and sew the seam, finish the raw edges together using a serger or zigzag stitch, press the seam allowances to one side, with the right side facing up, sew an edgestitch close to the seam, then add a topstitch.

Tip: You can trim one seam allowance to reduce bulk if needed.

items.title: French seam
items.description:
Sew the pieces wrong sides together with a 4–5 mm seam allowance (You can also sew with a wider seam allowance and then trim it to 4–5 mm). Press the seam allowance to one side.
Fold the fabric along the seam so right sides are together, and press again.
Sew a second seam with a 7–8 mm seam allowance, enclosing the raw edge inside.

A French seam is a sewing technique often used for lightweight or sheer fabrics. It creates a clean, durable, and neat finish by enclosing the raw edges within the seam, resulting in a smooth, professional look on both sides of the garment.

items.title: Flat Felled Seam
items.description:
Place the pieces right sides together. One seam allowance is 1 cm, and the other is 2 cm.
Fold the wider seam allowance over the narrower one and sew a row of stitches close to the raw edge. Then Fold the entire seam allowance to one side so the raw edge is hidden inside.
After pressing, sew an edgestitch and a topstitch from the right side, making sure to catch the folded seam allowance underneath.
Press and done!

A flat felled seam is a super strong, and professional looking seam inside and out, often used in jeans and shirts. It’s durable and leaves no raw edges, perfect for garments that need strength and a neat finish!

items.title: Two-hole button #1
items.description:
Thread the cut ends or the looped end into the eye of the needle, making sure the folded end is at the bottom.

Insert the needle from the wrong side to the right side of the fabric, passing it through one hole of the button—but don’t pull it all the way through. Leave a small loop on the wrong side.

Now, bring the needle from the right side to the wrong side through the second hole of the button. Pass the needle and thread through the loop on the wrong side, then pull to tighten it.

This method helps you avoid knots on the back of your project.

To complete sewing the two-hole button, watch the next parts in this series:
#2 two-hole button, how to sew 
#3 two-hole button, how to finish

items.title: Two-hole button #2
items.description:
This is part two, don't forget to watch part one #1 how to start sewing a two-hole button on my account.

After starting, use a thick needle and place it between the first stitch and the button to create a small space between the button and the fabric. This space is essential — it helps the button sit perfectly when it’s closed into the buttonhole.
Sew three times using a double-threaded needle, and we’re ready to finish it.

Watch the next part to learn how to finish:
#3 two-hole button, how to finish

items.title: Two-hole button #3
items.description:
This is part three, don't forget to watch part one #1 how to start sewing a two-hole button, and part two #2 how to sew a two-hole button on my account.

After sewing the button in place, finish it on the wrong side of the garment: Pass the needle through a stitch to make a loop, then pass the needle through the loop and pull to tighten. Repeat one more time to make the knots secure. Then, pass the needle between the fabric layers, push the fabric down along the thread with your fingers, and cut it short. Straighten the fabric — the small thread tail will be hidden inside, invisible.

Now it’s time to remove the thick needle… and done! You just sewed the most perfect button!

items.title: 3 Tips to Sew a Button
items.description:
3 Tips to Sew a Button with a Clean Finish on Both Sides:
1. Start without a knot — you can watch the “Start Without a Knot” tutorial on my account.
2. Keep an eye on where you insert the needle.
3. Hide the thread end after knotting it off.

items.title: Four-hole button #1
items.description:
How to sew a button #1 how to start sewing a four-hole button 
To complete sewing the four-hole button, watch the next parts in this series:
#2 four-hole button, how to sew 
#3 four-hole button, how to finish

items.title: Four-hole button #2
items.description:
How to sew a button #2 how to sew a four-hole button 
To complete sewing the four-hole button, watch the next part in this series:
#3 four-hole button, how to finish

items.title: Four-hole button #3
items.description:
How to sew a button #3 how to finish sewing a four-hole button 
Don’t forget to watch Parts One and Two to sew a four-hole button. Love you

items.title: Shank button #1
items.description:
How to sew a shank button: button sewing techniques you need to know.
Please don't forget to watch Part 2 to finish sewing a shank button

items.title: Shank button #2
items.description:
How to sew a shank button: button sewing techniques you need to know.
Watch Part 1 to see how to start sewing a shank button

items.title: Double Fold Hem
items.description: Double Fold Hem is a method of folding the fabric edge twice: first, fold the raw edge inward by about 0.5–1 cm; second, fold it again by a larger amount (1–2 cm) to enclose the raw edge completely inside, then sew a line close to the inner fold or along the outer edge to create a clean and sturdy hem.

items.title: Single Fold Hem
items.description: Fold the raw edge once and sew straight; raw edge is visible inside unless finished with zigzag or serger.

items.title: Blind Hem - Hand
items.description: Fold fabric back, catch only a few threads of main fabric with a tiny stitch, then move diagonally; nearly invisible from the right side.

items.title: Blind Hem - Machine
items.description: Use a blind hem foot and zigzag-like stitch; fabric is folded with a crease, the machine takes a tiny bite of the fold, creating an almost invisible hem from the front.

items.title: Rolled Hem
items.description: Fabric edge is rolled tightly and sewn continuously (often with a rolled hem foot), creating a very narrow, smooth, rounded edge.

items.title: Narrow Hem
items.description: Very small double fold, sewn close to the inner edge; used on lightweight fabrics like chiffon or handkerchiefs.

items.title: Curved Hem
items.description: For rounded edges, use a narrow double fold, clip seam allowance at curves, press carefully, and sew slowly to avoid puckers.

items.title: Hemming Pants
items.description: Measure desired length, cut excess, fold twice (single or double), press, sew with straight stitch or blind hem; for jeans, keep the original worn edge by folding and topstitching with heavy thread.

items.title: Hemming Skirts
items.description: Depends on shape: straight skirts - double fold; flared/circle - narrow or rolled hem; use blind stitch or machine hem, pressing after each fold.

items.title: Hemming Sleeves
items.description: Usually narrow double fold or rolled hem (for thin fabrics), press carefully around the curve, sew with straight or blind stitch, keep even width all around.

items.title: Centered Zipper
items.description: The stitching runs straight down the middle, with fabric evenly covering both sides of the zipper. Sew from bottom to top using a standard zipper foot, then stitch a bar tack at the bottom.

items.title: Invisible Zipper
items.description: The zipper is hidden inside the seam, with only the teeth visible. Use an invisible zipper foot, open the zipper, place it face down on the right side of the fabric, and sew close to the teeth. When finished, the zipper is completely concealed.

items.title: Lapped Zipper
items.description: One side of the fabric overlaps the zipper (lapped), while the other side lies underneath. Topstitch close to the folded edge on the upper side, creating a clean finish, commonly used for skirts with waistbands.

items.title: Exposed Zipper
items.description: The zipper is fully exposed on the outside, often used as a design feature. Baste the seam first, press it open, place the zipper face up on the right side of the fabric, and stitch close to both sides of the teeth.

items.title: Sewing Zipper with Lining
items.description: Sew the zipper to the main fabric first, then attach the lining over it to cover the back of the zipper. Alternatively, sandwich the zipper between the main fabric and lining (bag method).

items.title: Fixing a Broken Zipper
items.description: If teeth separate, gently squeeze the top of the slider with pliers. If the slider falls off, reattach it from the top. If teeth are broken, replacement is needed. If fabric is stuck, gently pull back or rub with soap.

items.title: Shortening a Zipper
items.description: Mark the new length, sew a bar tack across the teeth at that point (by machine or hand), then cut off the excess zipper below the tack. Reattach the top stop or leave the original top stop in place.

items.title: Elastic Waistband
items.description: Cut a fabric piece twice the width of the elastic, sew into a tube, insert the elastic, sew the elastic ends together, then stitch the elastic in place (or sew channels). No zipper needed.

items.title: Drawstring Waistband
items.description: Sew an attached or separate waistband, leaving two small openings at the front (or sides). Thread a drawstring through one opening, go around the waistband, and exit through the other opening. Can combine with elastic.

items.title: Basic Waistband
items.description: A separate waistband, usually with interfacing. Fold lengthwise, place right side onto the garment, sew along the seam, turn right side out, press, and topstitch close to the bottom edge.

items.title: Contour Waistband
items.description: A curved waistband, higher at the back and lower at the front. Cut in a curved shape with interfacing, sewn like a basic waistband but requires pressing curves to fit the body closely.

items.title: Gathered Waistband
items.description: The garment body is wider than the waistband. Sew two long basting stitches (or use elastic thread), pull the threads to create even gathers. Then attach the waistband (usually elastic or basic) onto the gathered section.

items.title: Adding Elastic to Existing Clothes
items.description: Make two small slits on the inside of the waistband (at the sides). Thread elastic through one slit, go around the waistband, and exit the other slit. Sew the elastic ends together, then close the slits. Alternatively, remove the old waistband and sew a new elastic waistband.

items.title: Fix Loose Waistband
items.description: For elastic waistband: make a small slit, cut a section of elastic shorter, sew the ends back together, close the slit. For rigid waistband: add darts at the sides or back, or sew small elastic pieces inside the side seams.

items.title: Patch Pocket
items.description: Cut a fabric piece in pocket shape, fold the edges inward 0.5-1 cm, press, then topstitch it onto the main fabric. Corners can be reinforced or decorative stitching added.

items.title: Inseam Pocket
items.description: Use two pocket lining pieces, sandwich them between the right sides of front and back panels, sew along the side seam. When finished, the pocket is completely inside, with only the opening visible at the side seam.

items.title: Side Seam Pocket
items.description: Similar to inseam pocket but the opening is topstitched with a curve or straight line, commonly seen in dress pants or skirts. Part of the pocket may be visible, finished with a facing or binding.

items.title: Welt Pocket
items.description: A slit pocket with a narrow welt covering the opening. Cut a slit on the main fabric, sew the welt and pocket lining, turn right side out, and bar tack the ends. Looks neat, often on blazers or dress pants.

items.title: Zipper Pocket
items.description: Cut a slit on the fabric, sew a zipper onto the wrong side, then attach the pocket bag underneath. Finish with binding or facing to cover the zipper edges. Keeps items secure, often used on sportswear or backpacks.

items.title: Cargo Pocket
items.description: A large patch pocket, often with a flap, with pleats at the bottom or sides to create volume (box shape). Topstitched onto pants or jackets, commonly seen on cargo pants or hunting jackets.

items.title: Add Pocket to Existing Clothes
items.description: Choose matching or contrasting fabric, cut a patch pocket, fold edges, press. Pin onto the desired location, topstitch close to the edge. Optionally add a zipper or flap. Avoid sewing over existing seams to prevent bulk.

items.title: Reinforce Pocket Corners
items.description: When sewing patch pockets or slit pocket openings, stitch a triangle tack or bar tack at each corner. Alternatively, use dense zigzag stitching or attach rivets to prevent the corners from tearing.

items.title: Set-in Sleeve
items.description: A sleeve sewn separately and set into a closed armhole. The sleeve cap is slightly eased to fit the armhole. Attach around the armhole, then sew the underarm seam. Creates a structured, tailored fit.

items.title: Flat Sleeve Attachment
items.description: Attach the sleeve to the flat (unsewn) armhole first, right sides together. Then sew the side seam and underarm seam in one continuous line. Easier than set-in sleeves, commonly used for casual garments.

items.title: Gathered Sleeve
items.description: The sleeve cap is wider than the armhole and gathered to fit. Sew basting stitches, pull to create gathers, then set into the armhole. Adds soft volume at the shoulder.

items.title: Puff Sleeve
items.description: A sleeve with fullness at the cap, cuff, or both. Gather or pleat the fabric, then attach to the armhole and finish with a cuff or elastic. Creates a rounded, voluminous shape.

items.title: Sewing Sleeve Cuff
items.description: Attach a cuff to finish the sleeve edge. Sew the cuff to the sleeve, fold it over to enclose the seam, then topstitch. Interfacing is often used for structure. Buttons or closures can be added.

items.title: Short Sleeve Hem
items.description: Finish the sleeve edge with a narrow or double fold hem. Fold, press, and stitch close to the edge. Use a rolled hem for lightweight or flared sleeves.

items.title: Adjust Sleeve Length
items.description: Mark the desired length, add seam allowance, and trim excess fabric. Finish with a hem or reattach the cuff. For sleeves with plackets, shorten both the placket and cuff proportionally.

items.title: Round Neckline
items.description: A curved neckline that follows the base of the neck. Cut along a smooth curve and finish with bias binding or a facing. Simple and versatile, suitable for many garment styles.

items.title: V-Neckline
items.description: A neckline with a sharp V-shaped point at the center. Mark the point precisely, sew to the tip, clip into the corner, then turn and press carefully to create a clean, sharp finish.

items.title: Square Neckline
items.description: A neckline with straight edges and right-angle corners. Clip into each corner before turning to reduce bulk, then press carefully to maintain crisp, clean edges.

items.title: Bias Binding Neckline
items.description: Finish the neckline using bias-cut fabric (cut at 45°) for flexibility. Sew the binding to the edge, fold to the inside, and stitch in place. Creates a clean finish that adapts well to curves.

items.title: Facing Neckline
items.description: Use a shaped facing piece to finish the neckline from the inside. Sew right sides together, clip curves, turn inward, and press. Provides a clean, structured finish, ideal for heavier fabrics.

items.title: Basic Shirt Collar
items.description: A structured collar made of a collar stand and collar fall. Sew the collar pieces right sides together, turn and press. Attach the collar to the stand, then sew the stand to the neckline, enclosing the collar. Creates a clean, structured look for shirts.

items.title: Peter Pan Collar
items.description: A flat, rounded collar that lies smoothly against the garment with no stand. Sew the collar pieces together, turn and press, then attach to the neckline using a facing or binding. Common in feminine or children's designs.

items.title: Mandarin Collar
items.description: A short standing collar without a fold-over section. Cut a slightly curved band with interfacing, fold lengthwise, and attach to the neckline. Often finished with buttons at the front for a clean, minimal look.

items.title: Attaching Collar to Garment
items.description: Pin or baste the collar to the neckline, right sides together. Attach a facing or collar band to enclose the seam. Clip curves, press well, and ease the fabric evenly to prevent twisting or puckering.

items.title: Fixing Uneven Neckline
items.description: Uneven necklines are often caused by stretching or inaccurate cutting. Remove the stitching, press the fabric flat, and re-mark the neckline evenly. Reattach the collar using pins, sew slowly, and avoid stretching. Use stay tape if the fabric has distorted.

items.title: Hook and Eye
items.description: A small fastening made of a hook and a loop (eye). Sew the hook to one side and the eye to the opposite side, aligning them carefully. Commonly used at waistbands, skirts, or collars where extra security is needed. Stitch firmly over the base to prevent loosening.

items.title: Snap Button
items.description: A press-fastener with two parts (ball and socket) that snap together. Attach using snap pliers or by sewing. For sew-on snaps, stitch around the holes securely. For metal snaps, insert prongs through the fabric and press firmly. Common in shirts, kidswear, and bags.

items.title: Velcro Closure
items.description: A hook-and-loop fastener with two sides: rough (hook) and soft (loop). Cut to size and sew onto fabric using a rectangle or cross-stitch pattern. Reinforce corners with bar tacks to prevent peeling. Keep away from raw edges to reduce lint buildup.

items.title: Button Loop
items.description: A small loop made from fabric or cord that fits over a button. Create using bias fabric or elastic cord, sew into a loop, then attach to the garment edge. Often used on delicate garments or back closures. Ensure the loop is snug for a secure fit.

items.title: Toggle Closure
items.description: A closure made of a toggle (bar) and a loop. Attach the toggle with a cord or fabric strip, and sew a loop on the opposite side. To fasten, pass the toggle through the loop. Common on coats and outerwear.

items.title: Sewing Clasps
items.description: Metal fasteners such as purse clasps or lobster clasps. Sew the base plate onto fabric using tight whipstitches, reinforcing multiple times. Use strong or nylon thread for durability. Ensure the base is well secured to handle tension.

items.title: Gathering Fabric
items.description: Sew 2–3 rows of long basting stitches within the seam allowance. Leave long thread tails. Pull the bobbin threads to gather the fabric evenly. Distribute the gathers, then attach to another fabric piece (e.g., a waistband). Leave 0.5–1 cm seam allowance.

items.title: Pleats - Box, Knife
items.description: Knife pleat: all folds face the same direction. Box pleat: folds face each other, forming an “H” shape. Mark pleat positions, fold accurately, and press firmly before stitching. Secure pleats by stitching across the top edge (within seam allowance) or stitch down partially/full length depending on design.

items.title: Sewing Darts
items.description: Fold fabric along the dart line, right sides together. Sew from the wide end toward the tip, tapering gradually and sewing off the edge at the tip. Do not backstitch at the tip; instead, tie the threads. Press toward center or down.

items.title: Ruffles
items.description: Cut a strip 1.5–3× longer than the area. Sew gathering stitches, pull to create gathers, and distribute evenly. Attach to the main fabric. Avoid pressing flat to maintain volume. Use a ruffler foot or two rows of gathering stitches.

items.title: Tucks
items.description: Small, even folds stitched down their entire length, used for decoration or light elastic effect. Fold fabric vertically or horizontally, press firmly, sew a line parallel to the folded edge about 0.3-0.5 cm away. No gathering, just folding and stitching.

items.title: Shirring - Elastic Thread
items.description: Wind elastic thread onto the bobbin by hand (without stretching). Sew parallel rows 0.5–1.5 cm apart (usually on the wrong side). After sewing, apply steam or heat to shrink the elastic, creating even gathers.

items.title: Adding Volume
items.description: Increase fullness by adding extra fabric using pleats, gathers, or inserts (e.g., godets). Cutting on the bias also creates natural drape. For sleeves, increase sleeve cap height. For skirts, add width at the hem and control fullness at the waistband.
